figures.caption: Figure 4: Qualitative comparisons. Ours is the only method that can efficiently and accurately generate the local low-poly mesh, with seamless stitching and good alignment with nearby edge flows. Dark red faces indicate back faces (i.e., the opposite side defined by the vertex winding).
Table 1: Main results. All numerical values presented are percentages (%).

Table 3: Baseline preprocessing and evaluation setup. “Closed mesh” means the method requires a watertight manifold input. “Global” means the method operates on the entire mesh (not just the target region).

Table 2: Ablation study. Cumulative component ablation; each row adds one component to the previous configuration. † Full model (all components enabled). †† Values in parentheses report the no-edit reference lower bound for OvR and A-Overflow: the values obtained when the original target faces are used as the generated patch. The reference lower bounds are non-zero because OvR and A-Overflow metrics use a distance proximity threshold ϵovf (Alg. 3). ‡ Naive token-axis concatenation replaces the gated fusion. ♢ Each encoder branch uses independently sampled query positions instead of the shared set q. All values are percentages (%).

Figure 5: Ablation qualitative results. Settings A, D, and E correspond to configurations in Table 2. Red rectangles indicate overflow regions. Expanding the context to width 3 (A→D) reduces overflow, and the low-poly geometry encoder (D→E) further suppresses it while improving generation quality.

Figure 2: Naive global serialization conflicts with local editing (faces sorted in YX lexicographic order). (a) A fixed context A\B. (b, c) Two valid tilings of the same hole with different face counts and shapes. (d) Under global serialization, the positions and number of target tokens (bi, green) in the joint sequence are determined by the target geometry itself, which is unknown before generation: different tilings produce different sequences. (e) MeshFIM serializes A\B (pink) and B (green) independently, so that the sequence structure no longer depends on the unknown target geometry.
Figure 11: More results of our method. Our method is able to do accurate local editing on various types of low-poly mesh.

Figure 10: Brush editing interface. Left: low-poly mesh with target B (blue) and contextA\B (light blue). Back-facing faces are tinted dark red to make it easier for users to observe broken regions. Right: reference GT mesh. Top bar: brush size, mode (2D/3D), context width, and navigation controls.

Figure 3: MeshFIM pipeline overview. Given an input mesh with a target region B and surrounding context A\B, a shared point cloud encoder E with a common set of query positions q encodes two point clouds—the reference one Pgt and the existing low-poly mesh Plp (with B removed)—into position-aligned latents zgt and zlp. The mesh is serialized into a FIM token sequence: context tokens (with positional embeddings and boundary markers) followed by target tokens. An Hourglass Transformer autoregressively generates the new patch B′, conditioned on the fused latents ẑ via cross-attention. The output is stitched back seamlessly along the exposed boundary.
Table 4: Iterative repair statistics on 2560 generated meshes (max T =4 rounds).
Figure 9: The automatic defect detection pipeline. Our pipeline provides accurate defect detection to various cases via delicately designed graphics heuristics.
